does: This paper proposes RKLD, a reverse KL-divergence-based knowledge distillation algorithm designed to remove personal information from large language models while preserving their general capabilities. The method addresses the challenge of unlearning in LLMs by using knowledge distillation with reverse KL divergence to selectively forget target information about individuals while maintaining model utility on unrelated tasks. The approach is evaluated on unlearning personal data from LLMs, demonstrating improved balance between forgetting effectiveness and knowledge retention compared to existing gradient ascent-based methods that struggle with this tradeoff.
connects: This work extends the machine unlearning literature, which traditionally focused on small classification models where the information to forget was discrete and well-defined. It responds to emerging regulatory pressures like the Right to Be Forgotten and builds on knowledge distillation techniques used for model compression and transfer learning. The paper addresses limitations of gradient ascent-based unlearning methods that have been adapted to LLMs but often suffer from catastrophic forgetting or incomplete removal of target information. It connects to recent work on privacy-preserving machine learning and the challenge of handling continuous, distributed representations of personal information in large neural networks.
enables: This work opens research directions for privacy-compliant deployment of LLMs under regulatory frameworks requiring data deletion rights. It enables future work on efficiently unlearning other sensitive information types beyond personal data, such as copyrighted content or proprietary knowledge. The reverse KL divergence approach could be extended to selective model editing and targeted information removal without full retraining. The methodology may facilitate development of more nuanced unlearning strategies that distinguish between different categories of information to forget, supporting the practical deployment of LLMs in regulated domains where user privacy and data rights are critical concerns.
